metadata.title: A Christmas Carol Seat Crisis
metadata.curiosityNote: Live theater often improvises seating, turning props and spare furniture into extra seats at the last minute.

story.briefTemplate: For a school production of A Christmas Carol, the drama club had prepared {quantity:planned_folding_chairs} for the tickets they had sold. An hour before curtain call, the school's operations manager was called to the hospital for the birth of his third child. It was a joyful event, but without his keys to the supply closet, the crew could not get {quantity:inaccessible_chairs} in time. Then the theater teacher got creative and grabbed {quantity:bucket_stools} from their recent plant-a-tree drive for kids to use as stools in the front row, and many said they liked the closer view. Staff members offered to stand for the performance.
story.causalEvent: The operations manager had the supply-closet keys when he was called away to the hospital.
story.closingNoteTemplate: The seating log closed with {quantity:total_available_seats} ready for the audience.

label.child: Folding chairs set out for ticket holders
Chairs locked in the supply closet
Folding chairs they could actually use
All seats ready for the audience

steps.prompt: How many folding chairs could the crew actually use without the supply closet?
steps.reasoningPrompt: Some planned chairs were locked away. Did the number of usable folding chairs go up or down?

backwardCheck.prompt: Unlock the closet and add those chairs back. Do you return to the planned count?

steps.prompt: After the teacher repurposed the buckets, how many seats were available for the performance in total?
steps.reasoningPrompt: The available folding chairs and the bucket stools can both seat audience members. How do they make the total?

backwardCheck.prompt: Take the bucket stools back out. Do you land on the folding-chair count?

operatorExperiments.alternateWorldTemplate: This matches the story: the locked closet put chairs out of reach, leaving fewer to use.
Adding would fit a different story where {quantity:inaccessible_chairs} were delivered instead of locked away.
This would fit a different question: if there were {value:inaccessible_chairs} groups like {label:planned_folding_chairs.lowercase}, how many seats would there be?
This would fit a different question about sharing {label:planned_folding_chairs.lowercase} into equal groups.
This matches the story: {label:available_folding_chairs.lowercase} and {label:bucket_stools.lowercase} combine into every seat available.
Subtracting would fit a different story where {quantity:bucket_stools} were taken away instead of added to the rows.
This would fit a different question: if there were {value:bucket_stools} groups like {label:available_folding_chairs.lowercase}, how many seats would there be?
This would fit a different question about sharing {label:available_folding_chairs.lowercase} into equal groups.

recap.headline: Why the theater had {quantity:total_available_seats}
causalChain: The teacher added {quantity:bucket_stools}, making {quantity:total_available_seats} in all.

dataQuestion.prompt: What does {quantity:inaccessible_chairs} represent in the seating model?
dataQuestion.correctFeedback: Right. {quantity:inaccessible_chairs} is the number of planned chairs they could not reach.
dataQuestion.incorrectFeedback: That amount is the number of planned chairs they could not reach: {quantity:inaccessible_chairs}.
